title: BBQ pork ribs
subtitle: Oven-baked pork ribs with a spice rub and sticky barbecue glaze
excerpt: Pork spare ribs rubbed with a brown sugar and spice blend, wrapped in foil and baked low and slow until the meat pulls back from the bone, then unwrapped and glazed with barbecue sauce under a high grill until sticky and caramelised. The oven method delivers most of what a smoker provides without the equipment.
sourceNotes: Pork rib barbecue is a cornerstone of American barbecue culture, with regional variations spanning from the dry-rubbed ribs of Memphis to the sauced ribs of Kansas City. The low-and-slow cooking method over wood or charcoal is documented in American barbecue literature from the nineteenth century. The oven-foil method is a widely used domestic adaptation that produces results comparable to the smoked original.

content.text: The method is three stages: rub, bake wrapped in foil at low heat for 2 hours, then glaze and blast under a high grill for 10 minutes. The foil phase is the slow-cook that breaks down the connective tissue and makes the meat tender; the grill phase is what produces the sticky, caramelised exterior. Do not skip either.

Remove the membrane from the back of the ribs before applying the rub. The membrane is a thin, silvery skin on the bone side; if left on, it prevents the rub from penetrating and produces a chewy, leathery texture. Grip a corner with a piece of kitchen paper and pull. It usually comes off in one piece. The ribs are done when the meat has pulled back noticeably from the bone ends and a skewer inserted between the bones meets no resistance.

items.name: Pork ribs
items.prepNote: spare ribs or baby back ribs, membrane removed

content.text: Heat the oven to 150°C fan. Mix together all the dry rub ingredients. Pat the {{pork-ribs}} of ribs dry, then rub the spice mixture generously over both sides, pressing it into the meat. Lay the ribs meat-side down on a large sheet of foil. Wrap tightly in a double layer of foil and place on the baking tray.

Bake for 2 hours at 150°C fan. At the 2-hour mark, open the foil carefully, steam will escape. The meat should have pulled back from the bone ends and a skewer should slide between the bones without resistance. If not, rewrap and return for a further 30 minutes.

Remove the ribs from the foil and place on the baking tray. Brush the {{bbq-sauce}} of barbecue sauce generously over the meat side. Place under a hot grill, 10 to 15 cm from the element, for 5 minutes until the sauce is sticky and caramelised. Watch carefully, the sugar in the sauce burns quickly. Rest for 10 minutes, then cut into individual ribs to serve.

items.fix: Rewrap and return for another 30 to 45 minutes; check again, some racks take up to 3 hours
items.cause: The collagen has not broken down fully yet
items.symptom: Ribs are still tough after 2 hours

items.fix: Move the tray further from the grill element and watch constantly; the caramelisation happens fast
items.cause: Grill too hot or ribs too close to the element
items.symptom: Glaze burns under the grill before the ribs are sticky

items.fix: Seal the foil very tightly before baking, the steam trapped inside is what keeps the meat moist
items.cause: Foil not sealed tightly enough; steam escaped during the slow bake

items.fix: Apply the rub generously, the ribs should be visibly coated on both sides; pat dry before rubbing
items.cause: Too little rub applied, or ribs were too wet
items.symptom: Rub has not formed a noticeable crust

content.text: Pork rib barbecue is one of the most argued-over dishes in American cooking. Memphis, Kansas City, the Carolinas, and Texas all have their own rib traditions. They disagree on dry versus wet, the right sauce, and the best wood for smoking. What they share is the long, low cook that turns cheap, tough ribs into something tender. An oven cannot give you smoke. It can give you the time and the temperature, and the sticky, spiced, caramelised result is close enough to the real thing for most purposes.
